hero.tagline: Arizona's small claims courts offer a streamlined process for disputes up to $5,000, emphasizing speed and simplicity.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by court and claim amount

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Unless both parties agree in writing

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds $5,000, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. Instead, you must file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona allows you 6 years to file a claim for breach of a written contract and 3 years for an oral contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in 6 months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $3,500 to a colleague who agreed to repay in installments but has defaulted.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Gather any written agreements, payment records, and communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 but failed to make any payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and any records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $4,500 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $3,000 on their store credit account.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from last payment or charge. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for $1,200 worth of goods with a check that bounced.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of the check. Provide the bounced check and any bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + twice the check amount or $50, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you to pay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and proof of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona provides avenues for consumers to seek redress for various disputes, with specific statutes of limitations depending on the claim type.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of defect. Bring purchase receipts, warranty information, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $800 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Provide service agreements, receipts, and evidence of substandard work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue and caused further damage.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices, receipts, and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory work by a contractor
claims.example: A contractor was paid $4,500 for home renovations but left the work incomplete and subpar.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,000 for an event, but they never showed up.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Bring the service agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your card for 3 months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,000 that broke down within a week due to undisclosed issues.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales agreement, any warranties, and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $500 with features it didn't have, leading to your financial loss.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide the advertisement, purchase records, and communication with the company.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona allows 2 years to file claims for property damage, including vehicle collisions and damage caused by others.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident date. Bring police reports, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $4,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Bring the lease agreement, photos of damage, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,500 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property while in someone else's custody
claims.example: You left your laptop for repair, and the technician damaged it, causing $800 in losses.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Bring service agreements, photos of damage, and repair estimates.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona provides specific statutes for landlord-tenant disputes, with varying statutes of limitations depending on the claim.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection reports, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord didn't provide itemized list for deposit deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the deposit.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your rental had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from issue. Provide photos, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained personal property
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your furniture worth $2,000.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the property.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your rental.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from eviction. Provide the lease agreement and any communication regarding the eviction.

claims.name: Landlord didn't return prepaid rent after early termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent but moved out early, and the landlord kept the last month's rent.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the rent.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent, landlord disputes
claims.example: You paid $600 to fix a leaky roof and deducted it from rent, but the landlord disagrees.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona allows 1 year to file claims for unpaid wages and related employment disputes.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from when wages were due. Provide pay stubs and any communication regarding unpaid wages.
claims.damage_boost: Wages owed + 3 times the amount as liquidated damages.

claims.name: Employer didn't provide final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer withheld your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from termination. Bring employment records and any communication regarding the final paycheck.
claims.damage_boost: Wages owed + 3 times the amount as liquidated damages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from when commissions were due. Provide sales records and any communication regarding unpaid commissions.
claims.damage_boost: Wages owed + 3 times the amount as liquidated damages.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime but weren't paid the additional $800 owed.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from when overtime was due. Provide timesheets and any communication regarding unpaid overtime.
claims.damage_boost: Wages owed + 3 times the amount as liquidated damages.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona allows 2 years to file claims for personal injury and related personal disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped in a store due to a wet floor and incurred $2,500 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication regarding the return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle worth $800, and you have evidence of their involvement.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from theft date. Provide police reports and any evidence of the theft.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Arizona allows 2 years to file claims related to vehicle disputes, including mechanic issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from your apartment complex without proper notice, costing you $300 in fees.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from tow date. Provide towing receipts and any communication regarding the tow.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims courts in Arizona do not have jurisdiction over eviction cases.
Claims involving defamation are excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the small claims jurisdictional limit.
Divorce, child custody, and related matters are not handled in small claims court.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over claims against the state or its subdivisions.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in Arizona small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 2x check amount or $50, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + twice the check amount or $50, whichever is greater.
Wages owed + 3 times the amount as liquidated damages.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in small claims court unless provided by statute or contract.
damages.postjudgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at a rate of 10% per annum on judgments.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: If filed in the wrong venue, the defendant may file a motion to change venue, potentially delaying the case.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing depends on the specific Justice Court; check with the local court for options.

service_fees.notes: Varies by county; check with local sheriff's office.
Fees vary; contact local process servers for rates.

forms.name: Application for Deferral or Waiver of Court Fees

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (e.g., SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 150% of the federal poverty level
waiver.coverage_notes: Waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court for specifics.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If fees were deferred and you win, the court may order the losing party to pay the fees.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment against the loser.

entries.notes: Applies to actions for debt founded on a written contract.
Applies to debt not evidenced by a written contract.
Applies to contracts for the sale of goods under the Uniform Commercial Code.
Applies to actions for debt founded on a written contract.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
entries.notes: Applies to debt not evidenced by a written contract.
Applies to actions for personal injuries.
Applies to actions for injury to property.
Applies to relief on the ground of fraud or mistake.
Applies to actions to recover on a dishonored check.
Applies to actions for the recovery of unpaid wages.
Applies to actions for the return of security deposits.
Applies to actions to enforce a judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: For unpaid wage claims, a written demand is recommended to establish the claim before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from security deposits within 14 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: For consumer fraud claims, a written demand for relief is recommended before filing.

forms.name: Small Claims Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims lawsuit by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Small Claims Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's complaint.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant if they have a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Request for Transfer to Civil Division
forms.description: Used to transfer a small claims case to the civil division for a jury trial or attorney representation.

forms.name: Motion to Vacate Judgment
forms.description: Filed to request the court to set aside a judgment.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the prevailing party to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Court clerk sends documents via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual serves the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent; if none, serve an officer or managing agent.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Arizona rules and the rules of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and consider alternative methods like service by publication.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file a written answer within 20 days of being served.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to respond, the plaintiff may request a default judgment.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the party may appeal the denial within 10 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds $5,000, the case must be transferred to the civil division.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not allowed unless both parties agree in writing.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case to the judge or hearing officer.
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter at least 10 days before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or within 10 days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts may offer mediation services before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: Decisions in small claims court are final and cannot be appealed.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 10% per annum on unpaid judgments.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File an application for a writ of garnishment, serve the employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of garnishment, serve the bank, and the bank will freeze and remit funds from the debtor's account.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county recorder to create a lien on the debtor's property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; lien must be satisfied before property can be sold or refinanced.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, have the sheriff seize property, and sell it at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File an application for renewal before the judgment expires; no fee required.

debtor_exam_form.name: Application for Order to Appear for Examination

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized by the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a statement with the court.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to appear on their behalf.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for unlicensed work.

county_variations.detail: Maricopa County offers e-filing for small claims cases through its online portal.
Pima County provides free mediation services for small claims disputes.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: If your claim exceeds $5,000, the court will dismiss the case or require you to waive the excess amount.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim, excluding interest and costs, does not exceed the $5,000 limit.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can result in delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Arizona's rules for service of process carefully, using authorized methods.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of your claim.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Not bringing necessary evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can result in losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Failing to appear at the hearing
pitfalls.what_happens: If you don't show up, the court may dismiss your case or enter a default judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Mark your calendar and ensure you attend the scheduled hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring post-judgment procedures
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like garnishment and liens.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims monetary limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims cases increased from $3,500 to $5,000, allowing more disputes to be resolved in small claims court.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not allowed in Arizona small claims court unless both parties agree in writing.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases are typically resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Arizona small claims court is $5,000, excluding interest and costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: No, decisions in Arizona small claims court are final and cannot be appealed.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Arizona is valid for 5 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: Arizona small claims courts do not allow jury trials; all cases are decided by a judge or hearing officer.
Attorneys are prohibited from representing parties in small claims court unless both parties agree in writing.
There is no right to appeal a small claims court decision in Arizona; the judgment is final.
The statute of limitations for written contracts is 6 years, while for oral contracts, it is 3 years.
Small claims courts in Arizona do not have jurisdiction over eviction cases; these must be filed in the regular civil division.

interesting_findings: Arizona's small claims courts emphasize informal procedures to allow individuals to represent themselves effectively.
The recent increase in the monetary limit to $5,000 aims to make the court accessible for a broader range of disputes.
Despite the informal nature, strict adherence to procedural rules is essential to avoid case dismissal.

sources.label: Maricopa County Superior Court — Justice Courts
Ariz. Rev. Stat. Title 22 (Justice and Municipal Courts)
Ariz. Rev. Stat. Title 12 (Courts and Civil Proceedings)
Ariz. Rev. Stat. § 22-503 (Small Claims jurisdictional limit)
Arizona Rules of Procedure for the Justice Courts
Arizona Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4 (service)
